name: Maria Iasneva-Golubeva
note: Joined after the 1903 congress; died before the height of the Great Purge.

name: Anna Ulyanova
note: Lenin's sister; party member since 1898.

name: Peteris Stucka
note: Latvian Bolshevik and legal theorist; supported the Bolsheviks in 1903.

name: Pauls Dauge
note: Arrested during the Great Purge, but survived.

name: Nadezhda Krupskaya
note: Lenin's partner and a major party educator; died of illness.

timelineAnnotations.details: Joined Lenin in emigration after her release, living in Munich and then London before returning during the 1905 Revolution.
Left Russia again after the failed 1905 Revolution and remained abroad until 1917, including work in France.

howDied: Illness after a series of strokes.
name: Vladimir Lenin
note: Founder and leader of the Bolsheviks.

timelineAnnotations.details: After Siberian exile, moved to Western Europe and worked from Munich, London, and Geneva until returning during the 1905 Revolution.
Second long emigration after the defeat of the revolution; lived in European exile until returning after the February Revolution.

name: Leonid Krasin
note: Engineer, financier, and early Bolshevik organizer.

timelineAnnotations.details: After arrest in Finland in 1908 and release for lack of evidence, emigrated to Berlin and remained abroad until rejoining the Bolsheviks in early 1918.

name: Alexander Tsiurupa
note: Old Bolshevik and Soviet food commissar.

name: Semyon Sereda
note: Joined the Bolsheviks in 1903.

name: Gleb Krzhizhanovsky
note: Old underground comrade of Lenin and GOELRO planner.

name: Cecilia Bobrovskaya
note: Old Bolshevik organizer and memoirist.

name: Vladimir Bonch-Bruyevich
note: Administrator, writer, and early Bolshevik fixer.

timelineAnnotations.details: Emigrated to Switzerland in 1896, where he organized shipments of revolutionary literature and printing equipment back into Russia; returned in early 1905.

name: Elena Stasova
note: One of the longest-lived Old Bolsheviks in this selection.

timelineAnnotations.details: Emigrated to Geneva in August 1905 to run the Bolshevik organization abroad while Lenin was in Russia; returned to St. Petersburg in January 1906.

name: Sergey Gusev
note: Founding member of the Bolshevik faction in 1903.

name: Dmitry Ilyich Ulyanov
note: Lenin's brother and an old underground activist.

name: Mikhail Kalinin
note: Long-serving Soviet head of state in formal terms.

name: Maxim Litvinov
note: Diplomat and foreign commissar; survived Stalin's terror years.

timelineAnnotations.details: Escaped imprisonment in 1901 and moved to Geneva, where he worked for Iskra before returning to Russia during the 1905 Revolution.
Left Russia again amid arrests in 1906 and spent roughly a decade in emigration in Paris, Belfast, and London.

name: Rosalia Zemlyachka
note: Prominent revolutionary and Soviet official.

timelineAnnotations.details: After repeated arrests, emigrated in 1909 and remained abroad until her illegal return to Russia in 1914.

name: Joseph Stalin
note: Old Bolshevik and Soviet leader from the late 1920s until 1953.

name: Grigory Petrovsky
note: Old Bolshevik who outlived the purge years.

timelineAnnotations.details: Forced to flee after the 1905 Revolution and spent a brief period in Germany before returning to Mariupol in 1907.

name: Vladimir Adoratsky
note: Party intellectual and editor; survived the purges.

timelineAnnotations.details: After deportation to Astrakhan province, emigrated to Geneva and later lived in Paris, London, Berlin, and Munich before returning in 1918.

name: Serafima Hopner
note: Old Bolshevik who remained alive deep into the post-Stalin era.

timelineAnnotations.details: Lived in emigration from 1910 until returning after the February Revolution.

name: Mikhail Tomsky
note: Committed suicide before his show trial amid the purges.

timelineAnnotations.details: Arrested in 1908 and exiled to France; returned to Russia in 1909, was arrested again, and later sent to hard labour.

name: Nikolai Podvoisky
note: Revolutionary organizer and military figure.

name: Lydia Fotiyeva
note: The longest-lived figure in this selection.

name: Nadezhda Kolesnikova
note: Old Bolshevik active since 1904.

name: Lev Kamenev
note: Convicted at the Trial of the Sixteen and executed.

timelineAnnotations.details: Went abroad in 1908 to assist Lenin and remained in emigration until returning to St. Petersburg in 1914, when he was arrested and sent to Siberia.
Arrested again in December 1934 after the Kirov assassination, sentenced to prison in 1935, then retried at the first Moscow show trial and executed in August 1936.

name: Grigory Zinoviev
note: Executed in the first Moscow show trial.

timelineAnnotations.details: Fled police repression and lived in political exile until returning during the 1905 Revolution.
Left for Western Europe in 1908 and served as Lenin's close aide in exile until returning in 1917.
Arrested again in December 1934 after the Kirov assassination, given a ten-year prison sentence in January 1935, then retried in August 1936 and executed after the Trial of the Sixteen.

name: Aleksei Badayev
note: Worker deputy turned Soviet official.

name: Dmitry Manuilsky
note: International communist functionary who survived the terror.

timelineAnnotations.details: Escaped exile and reached Paris in 1907, where he remained politically active until returning to Russia in May 1917.

name: Andrei Bubnov
note: Arrested and shot during the Great Purge.

timelineAnnotations.details: Arrested by the NKVD on October 17, 1937, held through the purge investigations, and executed after a brief trial on August 1, 1938.

name: Dora Lazurkina
note: Spent 17 years in the Gulag and survived.

timelineAnnotations.details: Arrested in August 1937 during the Leningrad purge and initially sentenced to five years of exile before the sentence was converted into imprisonment in the Gulag in 1939.
Her 1937 sentence was converted into Gulag imprisonment in 1939. In 1949 the camp term gave way to exile in a settlement; she was released and rehabilitated in 1955.

name: Mikhail Frunze
note: Bolshevik commander who died well before the Great Purge.

name: Alexander Shliapnikov
note: Executed after breaking with Stalin's line.

timelineAnnotations.details: Emigrated in January 1908; lived and worked mainly in Paris until returning to Russia in April 1914.
Forced out again by the war in 1914; worked through Scandinavia, London, and a short U.S. fundraising trip before returning in late 1916.
Imprisoned in 1935 on alleged political crimes, refused to confess, and remained in custody until his execution on September 2, 1937.

name: Nikolai Krylenko
note: Old Bolshevik prosecutor and commissar, later executed.

timelineAnnotations.details: Fled possible re-arrest in early 1914, first to Austria and then to neutral Switzerland after the war began; Lenin sent him back to Russia in mid-1915.
Arrested on January 31, 1938, removed from office, and executed on July 29, 1938, after a rapid purge investigation and trial.

name: Sergei Kirov
note: His assassination became Stalin's pretext for mass terror.

name: Sergo Ordzhonikidze
note: Officially a suicide, widely read as tied to purge pressure.

name: Pavel Postyshev
note: Party boss later executed in the purges.

timelineAnnotations.details: Arrested on February 21, 1938, during the Great Purge, held for a year in custody, and executed on February 26, 1939.

name: Valerian Kuybyshev
note: Senior Soviet planner who died before the main show trials.
